other_observers: Nicholas Komar; I was the original finder and identifier.   David Leatherman and Rachel Hopper told me that they saw the bird later the same day. Tom, Cree and Skyler Bol reported to me that they saw the bird the next morning (10/6).
specific_location: Dixon Reservoir / Pineridge Natural Area 40 degrees 33 minutes 11.61 seconds North, 105 degrees 8 minutes 35.11 seconds West, from Google Earth
description: In the field, I saw that the bird was small, with light buffy sides and a whitish throat, with an often up-raised, barred tail, and a visible eye line, though not apparently as strong as expected for a Marsh Wren. Although I did not see more detailed marks in the field, the photos show streaking on the top of the head, consistent with Sedge Wren, without the brownish crown of Marsh Wren. I did not see details of the wings, nor do I have photographs where these are clear enough to be useful.Behaviors: The behavior of the bird was entirely consistent with Sedge Wren as described in Sibley. Though I was able to see the bird on the tree at the first sighting, this was the last decent look I got at it. The rest of the time I was listening to the bird and following it, it stayed low in the tall grass, though I could occassionally tell where it was by both the calling and movement of the grass. I spent, along with Nick Komar, whom I’d called, at least the next hour trying to photograph the bird again, and it stayed low in the grass nearly all of that time; we saw it quickly fly, low, only a handful of times. We were several times just feet from the bird, and it stayed down in the grass, often calling.Call: The call was a series of loud, clear, speparated and relatively "deep" chips, with the occassional multiples of the same. The call was a near-perfect match for the "chat calls_ND" entry in the _Sibley eGuide to Birds of North America_, Android version. For comparison, the call never had the "rattle" character of the Marsh Wren.Plumage: adult
photographer: Photographer: Brad Biggerstaff, Brad Biggerstaff, Brad Biggerstaff
